title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_31 × Z_25) prime?
generalization_signal: At p = 31 the fixed four-class barrier-threshold package survives almost exactly at its arithmetic boundary: the interval (29,174] contains exactly 30 = p - 1 usable primes for C, and the nontrivial multiples of 19, 23, and 29 occupy exactly 19 of the 20 A-slots. This makes the feeder strong evidence for a threshold-style slice or breakpoint theorem on Γ(Z_p × Z_25).
strongest_honest_claim: An explicit prime-labeling witness for Γ(Z_31 × Z_25) survives skeptical verification. The campaign-relevant gain is that this feeder closes at a near-sharp arithmetic boundary for the fixed barrier B = {1,19,23,29}: A absorbs all 19 nontrivial barrier multiples up to 174 with one slot to spare, and C can be taken exactly as the 30 primes greater than 29 in {1,...,174}. This is still feeder evidence rather than a proved family theorem, but it points to a real Γ(Z_p × Z_25) slice.
candidate_theorem_slice: A fixed-barrier sufficient-condition slice for Γ(Z_p × Z_25): if {1,...,5p+19} contains at least p - 1 primes greater than 29 and at most 20 nontrivial multiples of 19, 23, and 29, then taking B = {1,19,23,29}, A as the barrier-multiple reservoir, C from those primes, and D as the complement yields a prime labeling.
next_action: promote the verified p = 31 feeder into the family dossier, then run generalize before any Lean work and test whether z37-z25 is the first sharp obstruction to the current fixed-barrier wrapper
